Prince Hamlet of Denmark is the son of the recently deceased King Hamlet and nephew of King Claudius, who has taken the throne and married Hamlet’s mother Gertrude. Denmark has a long-standing feud with Norway after King Hamlet killed King Fortinbras in battle, and although Norway was defeated, Prince Fortinbras now threatens Denmark with invasion. On a cold night at Elsinore Castle, guards Bernardo and Marcellus discuss seeing a ghost resembling the late King Hamlet, filling them with fear and confusion.

Horatio joins the guards on the ramparts and witnesses the ghost himself, confirming that it truly resembles Hamlet’s dead father. The ghost disappears without speaking, leaving the men shaken and uncertain. They decide that Prince Hamlet must be told about the terrifying sight they have witnessed.

The next day at court, King Claudius discusses state affairs, permits Laertes to return to France, and sends messengers to Norway to restrain Prince Fortinbras. Claudius questions Hamlet about his grief and urges him to stop mourning his father. Queen Gertrude supports Claudius and encourages Hamlet to remain in Denmark, deepening Hamlet’s sense of betrayal.

After the court departs, Hamlet expresses despair over his father’s death and his mother’s hasty remarriage, feeling that life has become corrupt and meaningless. Horatio tells Hamlet about the ghost, and Hamlet resolves to confront it himself. That night on the castle ramparts, the ghost appears again and beckons Hamlet to follow it.

The ghost reveals that he is the spirit of Hamlet’s father and claims he was murdered by Claudius while sleeping. According to the ghost, Claudius poured poison into his ear and stole both his crown and his wife. The ghost commands Hamlet to avenge his murder but urges him to spare Queen Gertrude.

Hamlet swears to remember the ghost’s words and vows to seek revenge against Claudius. After the ghost vanishes, Hamlet decides to pretend to be mad in order to hide his true intentions. He makes Horatio and the guards swear never to reveal what they have seen or heard.

Polonius’s daughter Ophelia later tells her father that Hamlet visited her behaving strangely and appearing disturbed. Polonius believes Hamlet’s madness is caused by love for Ophelia and decides to inform the king and queen. Claudius summons Rosencrantz and Guildenstern, old friends of Hamlet, and asks them to spy on him.

Messengers from Norway inform Claudius that Prince Fortinbras has been restrained and redirected toward Poland, easing fears of invasion. Polonius confronts Hamlet, who mocks him with clever wordplay while continuing to pretend madness. Hamlet quickly realizes that Rosencrantz and Guildenstern have been sent to spy on him.

A troupe of traveling actors arrives at Elsinore, and Hamlet warmly welcomes them. He asks one actor to perform an emotional speech, which makes Hamlet reflect on his own inaction and weakness. Inspired by the performance, Hamlet devises a plan to expose Claudius’s guilt through a play.

Hamlet arranges for a performance that reenacts the murder of his father by poisoning. Polonius and Claudius spy on Hamlet again as Ophelia attempts to return his love letters. Hamlet delivers a soliloquy reflecting on life, death, and the nature of existence.

Hamlet harshly rejects Ophelia, convincing Claudius that Hamlet’s madness is not caused by love. The royal court gathers to watch the play staged by Hamlet. When the play shows a king murdered by poison poured into his ear, Claudius reacts violently and leaves the room.

Claudius’s reaction confirms his guilt in Hamlet’s eyes. Alone, Claudius attempts to pray for forgiveness but cannot truly repent while holding on to his stolen crown and queen. Hamlet finds Claudius praying but decides not to kill him at that moment.

Queen Gertrude summons Hamlet to her chamber to demand an explanation for his behavior. Polonius hides behind a curtain to spy on their conversation. During the argument, Polonius cries out, and Hamlet stabs through the curtain, killing him by mistake.

Hamlet realizes his terrible error but continues to accuse his mother of betrayal and moral weakness. The ghost reappears to remind Hamlet of his mission and urge him to be gentle with Gertrude. Unable to see the ghost, Gertrude believes Hamlet’s madness has worsened.

Hamlet urges his mother to repent and avoid Claudius, then drags Polonius’s body away. Claudius questions Hamlet about the body, but Hamlet responds with riddles and mockery. Fearing for his life, Claudius sends Hamlet to England with secret orders for his execution.

Ophelia descends into madness after her father’s death and wanders the castle singing sorrowful songs. Laertes returns from France furious over his father’s murder and his sister’s condition. Claudius manipulates Laertes and convinces him that Hamlet is responsible.

A plot is formed in which Laertes will duel Hamlet using a poisoned sword. Claudius prepares poisoned wine as a backup plan to ensure Hamlet’s death. Meanwhile, Ophelia drowns under unclear circumstances, leaving Laertes devastated.

During his journey to England, Hamlet discovers Claudius’s execution order and secretly alters it. He escapes death and returns to Denmark, reflecting on fate and accepting the inevitability of mortality. Hamlet feels prepared for whatever awaits him.

Two gravediggers joke about death while preparing Ophelia’s grave, treating mortality lightly. Hamlet arrives and discovers the skull of Yorick, a jester from his childhood. Holding the skull, Hamlet reflects deeply on life, death, and human equality.

Ophelia’s funeral procession arrives, led by Laertes in overwhelming grief. Hamlet reveals himself and declares his love for Ophelia. This leads to a physical fight between Hamlet and Laertes at the graveside.

Hamlet later explains to Horatio how Rosencrantz and Guildenstern were condemned by the altered letter. Osric delivers Laertes’s challenge to Hamlet for a fencing match. Despite Horatio’s concerns, Hamlet accepts the duel, trusting in fate.

The duel begins, and Hamlet scores the first points against Laertes with impressive skill. Queen Gertrude drinks poisoned wine meant for Hamlet and collapses. Claudius attempts to stop her but is too late.

Laertes wounds Hamlet with the poisoned blade, and during the struggle they exchange swords. Hamlet wounds Laertes with the poisoned weapon. As death approaches, Laertes confesses Claudius’s plot and asks Hamlet’s forgiveness.

Enraged and dying, Hamlet kills Claudius and finally avenges his father’s murder. As the poison spreads through his body, Hamlet names Fortinbras as the next king of Denmark. He urges Horatio to live and tell the true story.

Hamlet dies in Horatio’s arms, declaring that the rest is silence. Fortinbras arrives, claims the throne of Denmark, and orders a military funeral to honor Hamlet. The tragic story of the Danish royal family comes to an end.
